[[ja:%E5%B0%8F%E9%B6%B4%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:Shortest 100 pages as of 2025-02-13]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq-Kozuru Gion Festival-qq]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] {{Event information | Name = Koduru Gion Festival | English name = Koduru Gion Festival | Image = File:Kodurusyoutengai.jpg | Image size = 280px | Image caption = Procession of portable shrines and floats through Koduru Shopping Street | Type = Gion Festival (Sannō Festival) | Common name = Koduru Festival | Official name = | Former name = | Date = Saturday and Sunday of the three-day holiday weekend in July | First held = 1894 (oldest record found in Suwa Shrine documents) | Last held = | Time = | Venue = Koduru Shopping Street and surrounding area, Ibaraki Town, Higashiibaraki District, Ibaraki Prefecture | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Sponsor = | Support = | Partner = | Producer = | Number of exhibitors = | Attendance = | Venue access name = | Nearest station = [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] | Bus = From Mito Station, take the bus to "Koduru" stop. | Parking = Available | Website = | Notes = Until 2003, the festival was held annually on July 20–21. }}
The '''Koduru Gion Festival''' (小鶴祇園祭) is an annual [[Gion Festival]] held every July in the Koduru area of [[Ibaraki Town]], [[Higashiibaraki District]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]].
== Overview == The festival is a ritual of [[Yasaka Shrine]], which is located within the precincts of [[Suwa Shrine (Koduru)]]. It is said to have begun during the [[Meiji period]] when an [[epidemic]] spread through the area<ref>Suwa Shrine’s records show that the festival was held in [[1894]] (Meiji 27).</ref>. To pray for health and safety, a Yasaka Shrine was built within Suwa Shrine’s grounds, and the festival was initiated<ref>[http://www.nukaga-fukushiro.jp/report/098-2012/06-7-2/ Report by Fukushiro Nukaga, 2012/07/15]</ref>.
Although it declined once during the [[Taisho period]], young volunteers revived it, and since the [[1980s]], it has gained popularity once again<ref>“Ibaraki Town History - Geography Volume.”</ref>.
On the first day, the evening procession (宵祇園, Yoi Gion) begins when the portable shrine (mikoshi) leaves the shrine and reaches the temporary shrine (御仮屋, Okariya), where attendants keep an all-night vigil. On the second day, the main procession (本祇園, Hon Gion) parades through local neighborhoods and Koduru Shopping Street (along [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 16|Prefectural Route 16]]), accompanied by children's mikoshi and festival floats.
Until [[2003]], the festival was held annually on [[July 20]] and [[July 21]], regardless of the weekday. Since [[2004]], it has been held on the Saturday and Sunday of the three-day weekend that includes [[Marine Day]] (Umi no Hi).
On the festival days, a section of Koduru Shopping Street is closed to traffic, creating a pedestrian zone from 6:00 PM to 8:00 PM on the first day and from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the second day<ref>PR poster of the festival.</ref>.
== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** * 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** * 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) * 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** * **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." * **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. * **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

Temporary parking is available. * **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.

[[ja:%E5%B0%8F%E9%B6%B4%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]]
[[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:Shortest 100 pages as of 2025-02-13]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Kozuru Gion Festival-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] {{Event information | Name = Koduru Gion Festival | English name = Koduru Gion Festival | Image = File:Kodurusyoutengai.jpg | Image size = 280px | Image caption = Procession of portable shrines and floats through Koduru Shopping Street | Type = Gion Festival (Sannō Festival) | Common name = Koduru Festival | Official name = | Former name = | Date = Saturday and Sunday of the three-day holiday weekend in July | First held = 1894 (oldest record found in Suwa Shrine documents) | Last held = | Time = | Venue = Koduru Shopping Street and surrounding area, Ibaraki Town, Higashiibaraki District, Ibaraki Prefecture | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Sponsor = | Support = | Partner = | Producer = | Number of exhibitors = | Attendance = | Venue access name = | Nearest station = [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] | Bus = From Mito Station, take the bus to "Koduru" stop. | Parking = Available | Website = | Notes = Until 2003, the festival was held annually on July 20–21. }}
The '''Koduru Gion Festival''' (小鶴祇園祭) is an annual [[Gion Festival]] held every July in the Koduru area of [[Ibaraki Town]], [[Higashiibaraki District]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]].
== Overview == The festival is a ritual of [[Yasaka Shrine]], which is located within the precincts of [[Suwa Shrine (Koduru)]]. It is said to have begun during the [[Meiji period]] when an [[epidemic]] spread through the area<ref>Suwa Shrine’s records show that the festival was held in [[1894]] (Meiji 27).</ref>. To pray for health and safety, a Yasaka Shrine was built within Suwa Shrine’s grounds, and the festival was initiated<ref>[http://www.nukaga-fukushiro.jp/report/098-2012/06-7-2/ Report by Fukushiro Nukaga, 2012/07/15]</ref>.
Although it declined once during the [[Taisho period]], young volunteers revived it, and since the [[1980s]], it has gained popularity once again<ref>“Ibaraki Town History - Geography Volume.”</ref>.
On the first day, the evening procession (宵祇園, Yoi Gion) begins when the portable shrine (mikoshi) leaves the shrine and reaches the temporary shrine (御仮屋, Okariya), where attendants keep an all-night vigil. On the second day, the main procession (本祇園, Hon Gion) parades through local neighborhoods and Koduru Shopping Street (along [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 16|Prefectural Route 16]]), accompanied by children's mikoshi and festival floats.
Until [[2003]], the festival was held annually on [[July 20]] and [[July 21]], regardless of the weekday. Since [[2004]], it has been held on the Saturday and Sunday of the three-day weekend that includes [[Marine Day]] (Umi no Hi).
On the festival days, a section of Koduru Shopping Street is closed to traffic, creating a pedestrian zone from 6:00 PM to 8:00 PM on the first day and from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the second day<ref>PR poster of the festival.</ref>.
== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** * 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** * 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) * 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** * **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." * **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. * **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.

[[ja:%E5%B0%8F%E9%B6%B4%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:Shortest 100 pages as of 2025-02-13]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Kozuru Gion Festival--> [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] {{Event information | Name = Koduru Gion Festival | English name = Koduru Gion Festival | Image = File:Kodurusyoutengai.jpg | Image size = 280px | Image caption = Procession of portable shrines and floats through Koduru Shopping Street | Type = Gion Festival (Sannō Festival) | Common name = Koduru Festival | Official name = | Former name = | Date = Saturday and Sunday of the three-day holiday weekend in July | First held = 1894 (oldest record found in Suwa Shrine documents) | Last held = | Time = | Venue = Koduru Shopping Street and surrounding area, Ibaraki Town, Higashiibaraki District, Ibaraki Prefecture | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Sponsor = | Support = | Partner = | Producer = | Number of exhibitors = | Attendance = | Venue access name = | Nearest station = [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] | Bus = From Mito Station, take the bus to "Koduru" stop. | Parking = Available | Website = | Notes = Until 2003, the festival was held annually on July 20–21. }}
The '''Koduru Gion Festival''' (小鶴祇園祭) is an annual [[Gion Festival]] held every July in the Koduru area of [[Ibaraki Town]], [[Higashiibaraki District]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]].
== Overview == The festival is a ritual of [[Yasaka Shrine]], which is located within the precincts of [[Suwa Shrine (Koduru)]]. It is said to have begun during the [[Meiji period]] when an [[epidemic]] spread through the area<ref>Suwa Shrine’s records show that the festival was held in [[1894]] (Meiji 27).</ref>. To pray for health and safety, a Yasaka Shrine was built within Suwa Shrine’s grounds, and the festival was initiated<ref>[http://www.nukaga-fukushiro.jp/report/098-2012/06-7-2/ Report by Fukushiro Nukaga, 2012/07/15]</ref>.
Although it declined once during the [[Taisho period]], young volunteers revived it, and since the [[1980s]], it has gained popularity once again<ref>“Ibaraki Town History - Geography Volume.”</ref>.
On the first day, the evening procession (宵祇園, Yoi Gion) begins when the portable shrine (mikoshi) leaves the shrine and reaches the temporary shrine (御仮屋, Okariya), where attendants keep an all-night vigil. On the second day, the main procession (本祇園, Hon Gion) parades through local neighborhoods and Koduru Shopping Street (along [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 16|Prefectural Route 16]]), accompanied by children's mikoshi and festival floats.
Until [[2003]], the festival was held annually on [[July 20]] and [[July 21]], regardless of the weekday. Since [[2004]], it has been held on the Saturday and Sunday of the three-day weekend that includes [[Marine Day]] (Umi no Hi).
On the festival days, a section of Koduru Shopping Street is closed to traffic, creating a pedestrian zone from 6:00 PM to 8:00 PM on the first day and from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the second day<ref>PR poster of the festival.</ref>.
== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** * 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** * 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) * 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** * **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." * **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. * **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

[[ja:%E5%B0%8F%E9%B6%B4%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:Shortest 100 pages as of 2025-02-13]] [[Category:New pages]] <!--Kozuru Gion Festival--> [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] {{Event information | Name = Koduru Gion Festival | English name = Koduru Gion Festival | Image = File:Kodurusyoutengai.jpg | Image size = 280px | Image caption = Procession of portable shrines and floats through Koduru Shopping Street | Type = Gion Festival (Sannō Festival) | Common name = Koduru Festival | Official name = | Former name = | Date = Saturday and Sunday of the three-day holiday weekend in July | First held = 1894 (oldest record found in Suwa Shrine documents) | Last held = | Time = | Venue = Koduru Shopping Street and surrounding area, Ibaraki Town, Higashiibaraki District, Ibaraki Prefecture | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Sponsor = | Support = | Partner = | Producer = | Number of exhibitors = | Attendance = | Venue access name = | Nearest station = [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] | Bus = From Mito Station, take the bus to "Koduru" stop. | Parking = Available | Website = | Notes = Until 2003, the festival was held annually on July 20–21. }}
The '''Koduru Gion Festival''' (小鶴祇園祭) is an annual [[Gion Festival]] held every July in the Koduru area of [[Ibaraki Town]], [[Higashiibaraki District]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]].
== Overview == The festival is a ritual of [[Yasaka Shrine]], which is located within the precincts of [[Suwa Shrine (Koduru)]]. It is said to have begun during the [[Meiji period]] when an [[epidemic]] spread through the area<ref>Suwa Shrine’s records show that the festival was held in [[1894]] (Meiji 27).</ref>. To pray for health and safety, a Yasaka Shrine was built within Suwa Shrine’s grounds, and the festival was initiated<ref>[http://www.nukaga-fukushiro.jp/report/098-2012/06-7-2/ Report by Fukushiro Nukaga, 2012/07/15]</ref>.
Although it declined once during the [[Taisho period]], young volunteers revived it, and since the [[1980s]], it has gained popularity once again<ref>“Ibaraki Town History - Geography Volume.”</ref>.
On the first day, the evening procession (宵祇園, Yoi Gion) begins when the portable shrine (mikoshi) leaves the shrine and reaches the temporary shrine (御仮屋, Okariya), where attendants keep an all-night vigil. On the second day, the main procession (本祇園, Hon Gion) parades through local neighborhoods and Koduru Shopping Street (along [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 16|Prefectural Route 16]]), accompanied by children's mikoshi and festival floats.
Until [[2003]], the festival was held annually on [[July 20]] and [[July 21]], regardless of the weekday. Since [[2004]], it has been held on the Saturday and Sunday of the three-day weekend that includes [[Marine Day]] (Umi no Hi).
On the festival days, a section of Koduru Shopping Street is closed to traffic, creating a pedestrian zone from 6:00 PM to 8:00 PM on the first day and from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the second day<ref>PR poster of the festival.</ref>.
== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** * 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** * 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) * 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** * **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." * **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. * **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

[[ja:%E5%B0%8F%E9%B6%B4%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD]] [[Category:remove new pages from these]][[Category:C]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:Shortest 100 pages as of 2025-02-13]] <!--Kozuru Gion Festival--> [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] {{Event information | Name = Koduru Gion Festival | English name = Koduru Gion Festival | Image = File:Kodurusyoutengai.jpg | Image size = 280px | Image caption = Procession of portable shrines and floats through Koduru Shopping Street | Type = Gion Festival (Sannō Festival) | Common name = Koduru Festival | Official name = | Former name = | Date = Saturday and Sunday of the three-day holiday weekend in July | First held = 1894 (oldest record found in Suwa Shrine documents) | Last held = | Time = | Venue = Koduru Shopping Street and surrounding area, Ibaraki Town, Higashiibaraki District, Ibaraki Prefecture | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Sponsor = | Support = | Partner = | Producer = | Number of exhibitors = | Attendance = | Venue access name = | Nearest station = [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] | Bus = From Mito Station, take the bus to "Koduru" stop. | Parking = Available | Website = | Notes = Until 2003, the festival was held annually on July 20–21. }}
The '''Koduru Gion Festival''' (小鶴祇園祭) is an annual [[Gion Festival]] held every July in the Koduru area of [[Ibaraki Town]], [[Higashiibaraki District]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]].
== Overview == The festival is a ritual of [[Yasaka Shrine]], which is located within the precincts of [[Suwa Shrine (Koduru)]]. It is said to have begun during the [[Meiji period]] when an [[epidemic]] spread through the area<ref>Suwa Shrine’s records show that the festival was held in [[1894]] (Meiji 27).</ref>. To pray for health and safety, a Yasaka Shrine was built within Suwa Shrine’s grounds, and the festival was initiated<ref>[http://www.nukaga-fukushiro.jp/report/098-2012/06-7-2/ Report by Fukushiro Nukaga, 2012/07/15]</ref>.
Although it declined once during the [[Taisho period]], young volunteers revived it, and since the [[1980s]], it has gained popularity once again<ref>“Ibaraki Town History - Geography Volume.”</ref>.
On the first day, the evening procession (宵祇園, Yoi Gion) begins when the portable shrine (mikoshi) leaves the shrine and reaches the temporary shrine (御仮屋, Okariya), where attendants keep an all-night vigil. On the second day, the main procession (本祇園, Hon Gion) parades through local neighborhoods and Koduru Shopping Street (along [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 16|Prefectural Route 16]]), accompanied by children's mikoshi and festival floats.
Until [[2003]], the festival was held annually on [[July 20]] and [[July 21]], regardless of the weekday. Since [[2004]], it has been held on the Saturday and Sunday of the three-day weekend that includes [[Marine Day]] (Umi no Hi).
On the festival days, a section of Koduru Shopping Street is closed to traffic, creating a pedestrian zone from 6:00 PM to 8:00 PM on the first day and from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the second day<ref>PR poster of the festival.</ref>.
== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** * 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** * 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) * 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** * **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." * **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. * **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

[[ja:%E5%B0%8F%E9%B6%B4%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD]] [[Category:remove new pages from these]][[Category:C]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]]<!--Kozuru Gion Festival--> [[Category:qq]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:qq]] {{Event information | Name = Koduru Gion Festival | English name = Koduru Gion Festival | Image = File:Kodurusyoutengai.jpg | Image size = 280px | Image caption = Procession of portable shrines and floats through Koduru Shopping Street | Type = Gion Festival (Sannō Festival) | Common name = Koduru Festival | Official name = | Former name = | Date = Saturday and Sunday of the three-day holiday weekend in July | First held = 1894 (oldest record found in Suwa Shrine documents) | Last held = | Time = | Venue = Koduru Shopping Street and surrounding area, Ibaraki Town, Higashiibaraki District, Ibaraki Prefecture | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Sponsor = | Support = | Partner = | Producer = | Number of exhibitors = | Attendance = | Venue access name = | Nearest station = [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] | Bus = From Mito Station, take the bus to "Koduru" stop. | Parking = Available | Website = | Notes = Until 2003, the festival was held annually on July 20–21. }}
The '''Koduru Gion Festival''' (小鶴祇園祭) is an annual [[Gion Festival]] held every July in the Koduru area of [[Ibaraki Town]], [[Higashiibaraki District]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]].
== Overview == The festival is a ritual of [[Yasaka Shrine]], which is located within the precincts of [[Suwa Shrine (Koduru)]]. It is said to have begun during the [[Meiji period]] when an [[epidemic]] spread through the area<ref>Suwa Shrine’s records show that the festival was held in [[1894]] (Meiji 27).</ref>. To pray for health and safety, a Yasaka Shrine was built within Suwa Shrine’s grounds, and the festival was initiated<ref>[http://www.nukaga-fukushiro.jp/report/098-2012/06-7-2/ Report by Fukushiro Nukaga, 2012/07/15]</ref>.
Although it declined once during the [[Taisho period]], young volunteers revived it, and since the [[1980s]], it has gained popularity once again<ref>“Ibaraki Town History - Geography Volume.”</ref>.
On the first day, the evening procession (宵祇園, Yoi Gion) begins when the portable shrine (mikoshi) leaves the shrine and reaches the temporary shrine (御仮屋, Okariya), where attendants keep an all-night vigil. On the second day, the main procession (本祇園, Hon Gion) parades through local neighborhoods and Koduru Shopping Street (along [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 16|Prefectural Route 16]]), accompanied by children's mikoshi and festival floats.
Until [[2003]], the festival was held annually on [[July 20]] and [[July 21]], regardless of the weekday. Since [[2004]], it has been held on the Saturday and Sunday of the three-day weekend that includes [[Marine Day]] (Umi no Hi).
On the festival days, a section of Koduru Shopping Street is closed to traffic, creating a pedestrian zone from 6:00 PM to 8:00 PM on the first day and from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the second day<ref>PR poster of the festival.</ref>.
== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** * 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** * 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) * 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** * **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." * **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. * **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

The '''Koduru Gion Festival''' (小鶴祇園祭) is an annual [[Gion Festival]] held every July in the Koduru area of [[Ibaraki Town]], [[Higashiibaraki District]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]].
== Overview == The festival is a ritual of [[Yasaka Shrine]], which is located within the precincts of [[Suwa Shrine (Koduru)]]. It is said to have begun during the [[Meiji period]] when an [[epidemic]] spread through the area<ref>Suwa Shrine’s records show that the festival was held in [[1894]] (Meiji 27).</ref>. To pray for health and safety, a Yasaka Shrine was built within Suwa Shrine’s grounds, and the festival was initiated<ref>[http://www.nukaga-fukushiro.jp/report/098-2012/06-7-2/ Report by Fukushiro Nukaga, 2012/07/15]</ref>.
Although it declined once during the [[Taisho period]], young volunteers revived it, and since the [[1980s]], it has gained popularity once again<ref>“Ibaraki Town History - Geography Volume.”</ref>.
On the first day, the evening procession (宵祇園, Yoi Gion) begins when the portable shrine (mikoshi) leaves the shrine and reaches the temporary shrine (御仮屋, Okariya), where attendants keep an all-night vigil. On the second day, the main procession (本祇園, Hon Gion) parades through local neighborhoods and Koduru Shopping Street (along [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 16|Prefectural Route 16]]), accompanied by children's mikoshi and festival floats.
Until [[2003]], the festival was held annually on [[July 20]] and [[July 21]], regardless of the weekday. Since [[2004]], it has been held on the Saturday and Sunday of the three-day weekend that includes [[Marine Day]] (Umi no Hi).
On the festival days, a section of Koduru Shopping Street is closed to traffic, creating a pedestrian zone from 6:00 PM to 8:00 PM on the first day and from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the second day<ref>PR poster of the festival.</ref>.
== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** * 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** * 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) * 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** * **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." * **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. * **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />
{{DEFAULTSORT:Koduru Gion Festival}} [[Category:Festivals in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Gion Festivals]] [[Category:Ibaraki Town]]
[[sn:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:11]] {{pref-stub|Ibaraki Prefecture}} {{Event-stub}} [[Category:11]] [[sn:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:11]]

The '''Koduru Gion Festival''' (小鶴祇園祭) is an annual [[Gion Festival]] held every July in the Koduru area of [[Ibaraki Town]], [[Higashiibaraki District]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]].
== Overview == The festival is a ritual of [[Yasaka Shrine]], which is located within the precincts of [[Suwa Shrine (Koduru)]]. It is said to have begun during the [[Meiji period]] when an [[epidemic]] spread through the area<ref>Suwa Shrine’s records show that the festival was held in [[1894]] (Meiji 27).</ref>. To pray for health and safety, a Yasaka Shrine was built within Suwa Shrine’s grounds, and the festival was initiated<ref>[http://www.nukaga-fukushiro.jp/report/098-2012/06-7-2/ Report by Fukushiro Nukaga, 2012/07/15]</ref>.
Although it declined once during the [[Taisho period]], young volunteers revived it, and since the [[1980s]], it has gained popularity once again<ref>“Ibaraki Town History - Geography Volume.”</ref>.
On the first day, the evening procession (宵祇園, Yoi Gion) begins when the portable shrine (mikoshi) leaves the shrine and reaches the temporary shrine (御仮屋, Okariya), where attendants keep an all-night vigil. On the second day, the main procession (本祇園, Hon Gion) parades through local neighborhoods and Koduru Shopping Street (along [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 16|Prefectural Route 16]]), accompanied by children's mikoshi and festival floats.
Until [[2003]], the festival was held annually on [[July 20]] and [[July 21]], regardless of the weekday. Since [[2004]], it has been held on the Saturday and Sunday of the three-day weekend that includes [[Marine Day]] (Umi no Hi).
On the festival days, a section of Koduru Shopping Street is closed to traffic, creating a pedestrian zone from 6:00 PM to 8:00 PM on the first day and from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the second day<ref>PR poster of the festival.</ref>.
== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** ** 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** ** 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) ** 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** ** **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." ** **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. ** **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

Until [[2003]], the festival was held annually on [[July 20]] and [[July 21]], regardless of the weekday. Since [[2004]], it has been held on the Saturday and Sunday of the three-day weekend that includes [[Marine Day]] (Umi no Hi).

== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** ** 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** ** 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) ** 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** ** **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." ** **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. ** **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

The '''Koduru Gion Festival''' (小鶴祇園祭) is an annual [[Gion Festival]] held every July in the Koduru area of [[Ibaraki Town]], [[Higashiibaraki District]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]].
== Overview == The festival is a ritual of [[Yasaka Shrine]], which is located within the precincts of [[Suwa Shrine (Koduru)]]. It is said to have begun during the [[Meiji period]] when an [[epidemic]] spread through the area<ref>Suwa Shrine’s records show that the festival was held in [[1894]] (Meiji 27).</ref>. To pray for health and safety, a Yasaka Shrine was built within Suwa Shrine’s grounds, and the festival was initiated<ref>[http://www.nukaga-fukushiro.jp/report/098-2012/06-7-2/ Report by Fukushiro Nukaga, 2012/07/15]</ref>.
Although it declined once during the [[Taisho period]], young volunteers revived it, and since the [[1980s]], it has gained popularity once again<ref>“Ibaraki Town History - Geography Volume.”</ref>.
On the first day, the evening procession (宵祇園, Yoi Gion) begins when the portable shrine (mikoshi) leaves the shrine and reaches the temporary shrine (御仮屋, Okariya), where attendants keep an all-night vigil. On the second day, the main procession (本祇園, Hon Gion) parades through local neighborhoods and Koduru Shopping Street (along [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 16|Prefectural Route 16]]), accompanied by children's mikoshi and festival floats.
Until [[2003]], the festival was held annually on [[July 20]] and [[July 21]], regardless of the weekday. Since [[2004]], it has been held on the Saturday and Sunday of the three-day weekend that includes [[Marine Day]] (Umi no Hi).
On the festival days, a section of Koduru Shopping Street is closed to traffic, creating a pedestrian zone from 6:00 PM to 8:00 PM on the first day and from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the second day<ref>PR poster of the festival.</ref>.
== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** ** 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** ** 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) ** 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** ** **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." ** **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. ** **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** ** **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." ** **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. ** **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />
