[[ja:%E5%B0%8F%E9%B6%B4%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD]] [[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[Category:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]] [[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]]<!--Kozuru Gion Festival--> [[Category:11]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Kozuru Gion Festival]][[Category:11]] {{Event information | Name = Koduru Gion Festival | English name = Koduru Gion Festival | image = File:Kodurusyoutengai.jpg | image size = 280px | image caption = Procession of portable shrines and floats through Koduru Shopping Street | Type = Gion Festival (Sannō Festival) | Common name = Koduru Festival | Official name = | Former name = | Date = Saturday and Sunday of the three-day holiday weekend in July | First held = 1894 (oldest record found in Suwa Shrine documents) | Last held = | Time = | Venue = Koduru Shopping Street and surrounding area, Ibaraki Town, Higashiibaraki District, Ibaraki Prefecture | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Sponsor = | Support = | Partner = | Producer = | Number of exhibitors = | Attendance = | Venue access name = | Nearest station = [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] | Bus = From Mito Station, take the bus to "Koduru" stop. | Parking = Available | Website = | Notes = Until 2003, the festival was held annually on July 20–21. }}
The '''Koduru Gion Festival''' (小鶴祇園祭) is an annual [[Gion Festival]] held every July in the Koduru area of [[Ibaraki Town]], [[Higashiibaraki District]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]].
== Overview == The festival is a ritual of [[Yasaka Shrine]], which is located within the precincts of [[Suwa Shrine (Koduru)]]. It is said to have begun during the [[Meiji period]] when an [[epidemic]] spread through the area<ref>Suwa Shrine’s records show that the festival was held in [[1894]] (Meiji 27).</ref>. To pray for health and safety, a Yasaka Shrine was built within Suwa Shrine’s grounds, and the festival was initiated<ref>[http://www.nukaga-fukushiro.jp/report/098-2012/06-7-2/ Report by Fukushiro Nukaga, 2012/07/15]</ref>.
Although it declined once during the [[Taisho period]], young volunteers revived it, and since the [[1980s]], it has gained popularity once again<ref>“Ibaraki Town History - Geography Volume.”</ref>.
On the first day, the evening procession (宵祇園, Yoi Gion) begins when the portable shrine (mikoshi) leaves the shrine and reaches the temporary shrine (御仮屋, Okariya), where attendants keep an all-night vigil. On the second day, the main procession (本祇園, Hon Gion) parades through local neighborhoods and Koduru Shopping Street (along [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 16|Prefectural Route 16]]), accompanied by children's mikoshi and festival floats.
Until [[2003]], the festival was held annually on [[July 20]] and [[July 21]], regardless of the weekday. Since [[2004]], it has been held on the Saturday and Sunday of the three-day weekend that includes [[Marine Day]] (Umi no Hi).
On the festival days, a section of Koduru Shopping Street is closed to traffic, creating a pedestrian zone from 6:00 PM to 8:00 PM on the first day and from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the second day<ref>PR poster of the festival.</ref>.
== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** ** 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** ** 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) ** 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** ** **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." ** **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. ** **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

revision.text:
<!--Kozuru Gion Festival--> {{Event information | Name = Koduru Gion Festival | English name = Koduru Gion Festival | image = File:Kodurusyoutengai.jpg | image size = 280px | image caption = Procession of portable shrines and floats through Koduru Shopping Street | Type = Gion Festival (Sannō Festival) | Common name = Koduru Festival | Official name = | Former name = | Date = Saturday and Sunday of the three-day holiday weekend in July | First held = 1894 (oldest record found in Suwa Shrine documents) | Last held = | Time = | Venue = Koduru Shopping Street and surrounding area, Ibaraki Town, Higashiibaraki District, Ibaraki Prefecture | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Sponsor = | Support = | Partner = | Producer = | Number of exhibitors = | Attendance = | Venue access name = | Nearest station = [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] | Bus = From Mito Station, take the bus to "Koduru" stop. | Parking = Available | Website = | Notes = Until 2003, the festival was held annually on July 20–21. }}
The '''Koduru Gion Festival''' (小鶴祇園祭) is an annual [[Gion Festival]] held every July in the Koduru area of [[Ibaraki Town]], [[Higashiibaraki District]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]].
== Overview == The festival is a ritual of [[Yasaka Shrine]], which is located within the precincts of [[Suwa Shrine (Koduru)]]. It is said to have begun during the [[Meiji period]] when an [[epidemic]] spread through the area<ref>Suwa Shrine’s records show that the festival was held in [[1894]] (Meiji 27).</ref>. To pray for health and safety, a Yasaka Shrine was built within Suwa Shrine’s grounds, and the festival was initiated<ref>[http://www.nukaga-fukushiro.jp/report/098-2012/06-7-2/ Report by Fukushiro Nukaga, 2012/07/15]</ref>.
Although it declined once during the [[Taisho period]], young volunteers revived it, and since the [[1980s]], it has gained popularity once again<ref>“Ibaraki Town History - Geography Volume.”</ref>.
On the first day, the evening procession (宵祇園, Yoi Gion) begins when the portable shrine (mikoshi) leaves the shrine and reaches the temporary shrine (御仮屋, Okariya), where attendants keep an all-night vigil. On the second day, the main procession (本祇園, Hon Gion) parades through local neighborhoods and Koduru Shopping Street (along [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 16|Prefectural Route 16]]), accompanied by children's mikoshi and festival floats.
Until [[2003]], the festival was held annually on [[July 20]] and [[July 21]], regardless of the weekday. Since [[2004]], it has been held on the Saturday and Sunday of the three-day weekend that includes [[Marine Day]] (Umi no Hi).
On the festival days, a section of Koduru Shopping Street is closed to traffic, creating a pedestrian zone from 6:00 PM to 8:00 PM on the first day and from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the second day<ref>PR poster of the festival.</ref>.
== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** ** 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** ** 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) ** 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** ** **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." ** **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. ** **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

On the festival days, a section of Koduru Shopping Street is closed to traffic, creating a pedestrian zone from 6:00 PM to 8:00 PM on the first day and from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the second day<ref>PR poster of the festival.</ref>.
== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** ** 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** ** 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) ** 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** ** **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." ** **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. ** **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

revision.text:
{{Event information | Name = Koduru Gion Festival | English name = Koduru Gion Festival | image = File:Kodurusyoutengai.jpg | image size = 280px | image caption = Procession of portable shrines and floats through Koduru Shopping Street | Type = Gion Festival (Sannō Festival) | Common name = Koduru Festival | Official name = | Former name = | Date = Saturday and Sunday of the three-day holiday weekend in July | First held = 1894 (oldest record found in Suwa Shrine documents) | Last held = | Time = | Venue = Koduru Shopping Street and surrounding area, Ibaraki Town, Higashiibaraki District, Ibaraki Prefecture | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Sponsor = | Support = | Partner = | Producer = | Number of exhibitors = | Attendance = | Venue access name = | Nearest station = [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] | Bus = From Mito Station, take the bus to "Koduru" stop. | Parking = Available | Website = | Notes = Until 2003, the festival was held annually on July 20–21. }}
The '''Koduru Gion Festival''' (小鶴祇園祭) is an annual [[Gion Festival]] held every July in the Koduru area of [[Ibaraki Town]], [[Higashiibaraki District]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]].
== Overview == The festival is a ritual of [[Yasaka Shrine]], which is located within the precincts of [[Suwa Shrine (Koduru)]]. It is said to have begun during the [[Meiji period]] when an [[epidemic]] spread through the area<ref>Suwa Shrine’s records show that the festival was held in [[1894]] (Meiji 27).</ref>. To pray for health and safety, a Yasaka Shrine was built within Suwa Shrine’s grounds, and the festival was initiated<ref>[http://www.nukaga-fukushiro.jp/report/098-2012/06-7-2/ Report by Fukushiro Nukaga, 2012/07/15]</ref>.
Although it declined once during the [[Taisho period]], young volunteers revived it, and since the [[1980s]], it has gained popularity once again<ref>“Ibaraki Town History - Geography Volume.”</ref>.
On the first day, the evening procession (宵祇園, Yoi Gion) begins when the portable shrine (mikoshi) leaves the shrine and reaches the temporary shrine (御仮屋, Okariya), where attendants keep an all-night vigil. On the second day, the main procession (本祇園, Hon Gion) parades through local neighborhoods and Koduru Shopping Street (along [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 16|Prefectural Route 16]]), accompanied by children's mikoshi and festival floats.
Until [[2003]], the festival was held annually on [[July 20]] and [[July 21]], regardless of the weekday. Since [[2004]], it has been held on the Saturday and Sunday of the three-day weekend that includes [[Marine Day]] (Umi no Hi).
On the festival days, a section of Koduru Shopping Street is closed to traffic, creating a pedestrian zone from 6:00 PM to 8:00 PM on the first day and from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the second day<ref>PR poster of the festival.</ref>.
== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** ** 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** ** 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) ** 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** ** **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." ** **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. ** **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

revision.comment: 22 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:小鶴祇園祭 up to rev 101464291
revision.text:
{{イベントインフォメーション |イベント名称 = 小鶴祇園祭 |英文表記 = Koduru Gion Festival |画像 = File:Kodurusyoutengai.jpg |画像サイズ = 280px |画像説明 = 神輿・山車の渡御が行われる小鶴商店街通り |種類 = 祇園祭（山王祭） |通称 = 小鶴の祭り |正式名称 = |旧名称 = |開催時期 = 7月の3連休の土日 |初回開催 = 1894年(諏訪神社の資料での最古記録) |最終開催 = |開催時間 |会場 = 茨城県東茨城郡茨城町小鶴　小鶴商店街とその周辺。 |主催 = |共催 = |後援 = |協賛 = |企画制作 = |協力 = |運営 = |プロデューサー = |出展数 = |来場者数 = |会場アクセス名 = |最寄駅 = [[水戸駅]] |バス = 水戸駅からバス「小鶴」下車。 |駐車場 = 有 |URL = |特記事項 = 2003年までは毎年7月20～21日開催 }} '''小鶴祇園祭'''（こづるぎおんさい）は、毎年7月に[[茨城県]][[東茨城郡]][[茨城町]][[小鶴 (茨城町)|小鶴]]地内で行われる[[祇園祭]]である。 == 概要 == 小鶴[[諏訪神社]]の境内にある[[八坂神社]]の祭礼で、明治時代に疫病が蔓延したことから小鶴諏訪神社の資料にて、[[1894年]]（明治27年）に祭礼が行われた記録が残っている。、諏訪神社の境内に八坂神社を建立し、無病息災を祈願したのが始まりといわれている[http://www.nukaga-fukushiro.jp/report/098-2012/06-7-2/　ぬかが福志郎の活動報告 2012/07/15] 。
その後、大正期に一度は廃れかけたものの、当時の青年有志が再建し、1980年代頃からは賑わいを見せるようになって現在に至る「茨城町史　地誌編」。 1日目の宵祇園では、神社を出た神輿が御仮屋に到着した後、当番人は翌朝まで御仮屋で寝ずの番をし、2日目の本祇園では地域や小鶴商店街（[[茨城県道16号大洗友部線|県道16号線]]）で渡御が行われ、子供神輿や山車も一緒に練り歩く。
日程は[[2003年]]までは毎年曜日を問わず[[7月20日]]～[[7月21日]]に行われてきたが、[[2004年]]からは7月の第3土日前後（[[海の日]]がある3連休の土日）の開催となっている。
祇園祭当日は小鶴商店街の一部が1日目:18～20時。2日目:18～22時の間は歩行者天国となる当祭のPRポスターより。 == 日程 == *1日目（宵祇園） :18:00 宮出し→神輿・山車渡御→20:00御仮屋到着 *2日目（本祇園） :8:00 御仮屋出発→神輿・山車地域渡御→13:00御仮屋到着(休憩)。18:00 御仮屋出発→神輿・山車渡御→19:30～21:30ステージイベント→21:45宮入り→22:00終了 *地域渡御は小鶴地内の各町内（宮本町（下宿）、中宿、第三、西町、南町、第四、バッケ、台町、一心）を回る。 *昭和時代中期までは、神輿・山車地域渡御は本祇園の深夜に行い、翌日、[[涸沼川]]でお浜入りを行ってから宮入りをしていた『小鶴まちかどギャラリー』2002年開催の『小鶴祇園祭の歴史』で、1954年の神輿お浜入りの写真展示。 *コロナ禍による中断前は2日目の本祇園は13時に御仮屋出発して地域渡御を行っていたが、熱中症対策のため、2023年からは午前中に地域渡御をするようになった。 == 山車 == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|小鶴ばやし山車　左が2018年落成の3代目山車。右が2014年落成の2代目山車]] 小鶴ばやし保存会による山車が2台存在する。囃子は大[[太鼓]]と締め太鼓、[[笛]]と[[鉦]]で演奏され、2台とも山車上段が踊り場、下段が演奏場となっている。踊り場は[[おかめ]]、[[ひょっとこ]]、[[きつね]]の3演目が行われる。 *[[1981年]]（昭和56年） - 初代山車が落成。[[2013年]]（平成25年）に老朽化に伴い引退するまで32年間使用された。 *[[2014年]]（平成26年） - 初代山車の後継として2代目山車が落成。地域渡御に使用されている。 *[[2018年]]（平成30年） - 初代山車をベースに改修した3代目山車が落成。小鶴商店街での渡御に使用されている。 == 神輿 == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|鶴祭會の神輿]] 鶴祭會（かくさいかい）による大人用神輿1基と、中学生用神輿1基、子供神輿3基が存在する。 *[[1982年]]（昭和57年） - 鶴祭會神輿完成。 == アクセス == *場所:茨城町小鶴　小鶴商店街地内（県道16号沿い） *交通: **電車、バス利用 : [[東日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[常磐線]][[水戸駅]]から[[関東鉄道]]バス奥ノ谷方面行きで「小鶴」下車。若しくは、[[東京駅]][[東京駅のバス乗り場|八重洲南口4番乗り場]]から[[高速バス]]『[[常磐高速バス#みと号|みと号]]』（県庁ルート）で、[[茨城町西インターチェンジ|茨城町西IC]]下車。当所よりタクシーで約10分。 **自家用車利用 : [[北関東自動車道]]茨城町西ICから[[茨城県道59号玉里水戸線|県道59号]]、県道16号で約10分。臨時駐車場あり。 **航空機利用 : [[百里飛行場|茨城空港]]から[[関東鉄道]]水戸駅行きで「小鶴」下車。 == 備考 == *[[1996年]] - [[2001年]]までは1日目に「小鶴よさこい祭り」というイベントがあった。 *[[2011年]]は祭りの4ヶ月前に発生した[[東日本大震災]]の影響で、神輿・山車の渡御が中止。規模を大幅に縮小して諏訪神社境内で神事のみ行った。なお、ステージイベントは翌[[2012年]]正月に代替開催を行った。 *ステージイベントは現在はものまね歌謡ショー→ビンゴゲーム大会→カラオケ大会の流れとなっている[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html 【茨城町】小鶴祇園祭協賛イベント]。なお、カラオケ大会は事前に申し込みをすればどなたでも参加できる。 *[[2020年]] - [[2022年]]は[[新型コロナウイルス感染症 (2019年)|新型コロナウイルス感染症]]の感染拡大防止のため、神輿・山車の渡御が中止。規模を最小限にして諏訪神社境内で密を避ける観点から当番人や祭典関係者の出席に限定して神事のみ実施した。 *[[2023年]]は、規模を一部縮小し、1日目の宵祇園を中止して2日目の本祇園のみ開催したPRポスターより。 *[[2024年]]は、2019年以来5年ぶりに通常規模で2日間開催したPRポスターより。 == 関連項目 == *[[祇園祭]] *[[いばらきまつり]] - 夏の部中止以前は当祭の神輿・山車も本祭に参加していた。また、「2012いばらきまつり」では、当祭のお囃子がステージイベントに参加している[http://www.town.ibaraki.lg.jp/DigitalArchive/files/Contents/20121015/0001.pdf 広報いばらきお知らせ版No.837　平成24年10月15日発行]。 *[[水戸黄門まつり]] - 毎年、当祭の神輿も参加している。 *[[三夜さん末広まつり]] - 鶴祭會万灯神輿として毎年参加している。 == 脚注 == {{DEFAULTSORT:こつるきおんさい}} [[Category:茨城県の祭り]] [[Category:祇園祭・天王祭]] [[Category:茨城町]] {{pref-stub|pref=茨城県}} {{Event-stub}}

その後、大正期に一度は廃れかけたものの、当時の青年有志が再建し、1980年代頃からは賑わいを見せるようになって現在に至る「茨城町史　地誌編」。 1日目の宵祇園では、神社を出た神輿が御仮屋に到着した後、当番人は翌朝まで御仮屋で寝ずの番をし、2日目の本祇園では地域や小鶴商店街（[[茨城県道16号大洗友部線|県道16号線]]）で渡御が行われ、子供神輿や山車も一緒に練り歩く。
日程は[[2003年]]までは毎年曜日を問わず[[7月20日]]～[[7月21日]]に行われてきたが、[[2004年]]からは7月の第3土日前後（[[海の日]]がある3連休の土日）の開催となっている。
祇園祭当日は小鶴商店街の一部が1日目:18～20時。2日目:18～22時の間は歩行者天国となる当祭のPRポスターより。 == 日程 == *1日目（宵祇園） :18:00 宮出し→神輿・山車渡御→20:00御仮屋到着 *2日目（本祇園） :8:00 御仮屋出発→神輿・山車地域渡御→13:00御仮屋到着(休憩)。18:00 御仮屋出発→神輿・山車渡御→19:30～21:30ステージイベント→21:45宮入り→22:00終了 *地域渡御は小鶴地内の各町内（宮本町（下宿）、中宿、第三、西町、南町、第四、バッケ、台町、一心）を回る。 *昭和時代中期までは、神輿・山車地域渡御は本祇園の深夜に行い、翌日、[[涸沼川]]でお浜入りを行ってから宮入りをしていた『小鶴まちかどギャラリー』2002年開催の『小鶴祇園祭の歴史』で、1954年の神輿お浜入りの写真展示。 *コロナ禍による中断前は2日目の本祇園は13時に御仮屋出発して地域渡御を行っていたが、熱中症対策のため、2023年からは午前中に地域渡御をするようになった。 == 山車 == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|小鶴ばやし山車　左が2018年落成の3代目山車。右が2014年落成の2代目山車]] 小鶴ばやし保存会による山車が2台存在する。囃子は大[[太鼓]]と締め太鼓、[[笛]]と[[鉦]]で演奏され、2台とも山車上段が踊り場、下段が演奏場となっている。踊り場は[[おかめ]]、[[ひょっとこ]]、[[きつね]]の3演目が行われる。 *[[1981年]]（昭和56年） - 初代山車が落成。[[2013年]]（平成25年）に老朽化に伴い引退するまで32年間使用された。 *[[2014年]]（平成26年） - 初代山車の後継として2代目山車が落成。地域渡御に使用されている。 *[[2018年]]（平成30年） - 初代山車をベースに改修した3代目山車が落成。小鶴商店街での渡御に使用されている。 == 神輿 == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|鶴祭會の神輿]] 鶴祭會（かくさいかい）による大人用神輿1基と、中学生用神輿1基、子供神輿3基が存在する。 *[[1982年]]（昭和57年） - 鶴祭會神輿完成。 == アクセス == *場所:茨城町小鶴　小鶴商店街地内（県道16号沿い） *交通: **電車、バス利用 : [[東日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[常磐線]][[水戸駅]]から[[関東鉄道]]バス奥ノ谷方面行きで「小鶴」下車。若しくは、[[東京駅]][[東京駅のバス乗り場|八重洲南口4番乗り場]]から[[高速バス]]『[[常磐高速バス#みと号|みと号]]』（県庁ルート）で、[[茨城町西インターチェンジ|茨城町西IC]]下車。当所よりタクシーで約10分。 **自家用車利用 : [[北関東自動車道]]茨城町西ICから[[茨城県道59号玉里水戸線|県道59号]]、県道16号で約10分。臨時駐車場あり。 **航空機利用 : [[百里飛行場|茨城空港]]から[[関東鉄道]]水戸駅行きで「小鶴」下車。 == 備考 == *[[1996年]] - [[2001年]]までは1日目に「小鶴よさこい祭り」というイベントがあった。 *[[2011年]]は祭りの4ヶ月前に発生した[[東日本大震災]]の影響で、神輿・山車の渡御が中止。規模を大幅に縮小して諏訪神社境内で神事のみ行った。なお、ステージイベントは翌[[2012年]]正月に代替開催を行った。 *ステージイベントは現在はものまね歌謡ショー→ビンゴゲーム大会→カラオケ大会の流れとなっている[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html 【茨城町】小鶴祇園祭協賛イベント]。なお、カラオケ大会は事前に申し込みをすればどなたでも参加できる。 *[[2020年]] - [[2022年]]は[[新型コロナウイルス感染症 (2019年)|新型コロナウイルス感染症]]の感染拡大防止のため、神輿・山車の渡御が中止。規模を最小限にして諏訪神社境内で密を避ける観点から当番人や祭典関係者の出席に限定して神事のみ実施した。 *[[2023年]]は、規模を一部縮小し、1日目の宵祇園を中止して2日目の本祇園のみ開催したPRポスターより。 *[[2024年]]は、2019年以来5年ぶりに通常規模で2日間開催したPRポスターより。 == 関連項目 == *[[祇園祭]] *[[いばらきまつり]] - 夏の部中止以前は当祭の神輿・山車も本祭に参加していた。また、「2012いばらきまつり」では、当祭のお囃子がステージイベントに参加している[http://www.town.ibaraki.lg.jp/DigitalArchive/files/Contents/20121015/0001.pdf 広報いばらきお知らせ版No.837　平成24年10月15日発行]。 *[[水戸黄門まつり]] - 毎年、当祭の神輿も参加している。 *[[三夜さん末広まつり]] - 鶴祭會万灯神輿として毎年参加している。 == 脚注 == {{DEFAULTSORT:こつるきおんさい}} [[Category:茨城県の祭り]] [[Category:祇園祭・天王祭]] [[Category:茨城町]] {{pref-stub|pref=茨城県}} {{Event-stub}}

revision.text:
{{Event information | Name = Koduru Gion Festival | English name = Koduru Gion Festival | image = File:Kodurusyoutengai.jpg | image size = 280px | image caption = Procession of portable shrines and floats through Koduru Shopping Street | Type = Gion Festival (Sannō Festival) | Common name = Koduru Festival | Official name = | Former name = | Date = Saturday and Sunday of the three-day holiday weekend in July | First held = 1894 (oldest record found in Suwa Shrine documents) | Last held = | Time = | Venue = Koduru Shopping Street and surrounding area, Ibaraki Town, Higashiibaraki District, Ibaraki Prefecture | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Sponsor = | Support = | Partner = | Producer = | Number of exhibitors = | Attendance = | Venue access name = | Nearest station = [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] | Bus = From Mito Station, take the bus to "Koduru" stop. | Parking = Available | Website = | Notes = Until 2003, the festival was held annually on July 20–21. }}
The '''Koduru Gion Festival''' (小鶴祇園祭) is an annual [[Gion Festival]] held every July in the Koduru area of [[Ibaraki Town]], [[Higashiibaraki District]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]].
== Overview == The festival is a ritual of [[Yasaka Shrine]], which is located within the precincts of [[Suwa Shrine (Koduru)]]. It is said to have begun during the [[Meiji period]] when an [[epidemic]] spread through the area<ref>Suwa Shrine’s records show that the festival was held in [[1894]] (Meiji 27).</ref>. To pray for health and safety, a Yasaka Shrine was built within Suwa Shrine’s grounds, and the festival was initiated<ref>[http://www.nukaga-fukushiro.jp/report/098-2012/06-7-2/ Report by Fukushiro Nukaga, 2012/07/15]</ref>.
Although it declined once during the [[Taisho period]], young volunteers revived it, and since the [[1980s]], it has gained popularity once again<ref>“Ibaraki Town History - Geography Volume.”</ref>.
On the first day, the evening procession (宵祇園, Yoi Gion) begins when the portable shrine (mikoshi) leaves the shrine and reaches the temporary shrine (御仮屋, Okariya), where attendants keep an all-night vigil. On the second day, the main procession (本祇園, Hon Gion) parades through local neighborhoods and Koduru Shopping Street (along [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 16|Prefectural Route 16]]), accompanied by children's mikoshi and festival floats.
Until [[2003]], the festival was held annually on [[July 20]] and [[July 21]], regardless of the weekday. Since [[2004]], it has been held on the Saturday and Sunday of the three-day weekend that includes [[Marine Day]] (Umi no Hi).
On the festival days, a section of Koduru Shopping Street is closed to traffic, creating a pedestrian zone from 6:00 PM to 8:00 PM on the first day and from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the second day<ref>PR poster of the festival.</ref>.
== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** ** 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** ** 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) ** 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** ** **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." ** **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. ** **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** ** 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** ** 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) ** 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** ** **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." ** **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. ** **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

{{Event information | Name = Koduru Gion Festival | English name = Koduru Gion Festival | image = File:Kodurusyoutengai.jpg | image size = 280px | image caption = Procession of portable shrines and floats through Koduru Shopping Street | Type = Gion Festival (Sannō Festival) | Common name = Koduru Festival | Official name = | Former name = | Date = Saturday and Sunday of the three-day holiday weekend in July | First held = 1894 (oldest record found in Suwa Shrine documents) | Last held = | Time = | Venue = Koduru Shopping Street and surrounding area, Ibaraki Town, Higashiibaraki District, Ibaraki Prefecture | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Sponsor = | Support = | Partner = | Producer = | Number of exhibitors = | Attendance = | Venue access name = | Nearest station = [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] | Bus = From Mito Station, take the bus to "Koduru" stop. | Parking = Available | Website = | Notes = Until 2003, the festival was held annually on July 20–21. }}
The '''Koduru Gion Festival''' (小鶴祇園祭) is an annual [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]] held every July in the Koduru area of [[Ibaraki Town]], [[Higashiibaraki District]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]].
== Overview == The festival is a ritual of [[Yasaka Shrine]], which is located within the precincts of [[Suwa Shrine (Koduru)]]. It is said to have begun during the [[Meiji period]] when an [[epidemic]] spread through the area<ref>Suwa Shrine’s records show that the festival was held in [[1894]] (Meiji 27).</ref>. To pray for health and safety, a Yasaka Shrine was built within Suwa Shrine’s grounds, and the festival was initiated<ref>[http://www.nukaga-fukushiro.jp/report/098-2012/06-7-2/ Report by Fukushiro Nukaga, 2012/07/15]</ref>.
Although it declined once during the [[Taisho period]], young volunteers revived it, and since the [[1980s]], it has gained popularity once again<ref>“Ibaraki Town History - Geography Volume.”</ref>.
On the first day, the evening procession (宵祇園, Yoi Gion) begins when the portable shrine (mikoshi) leaves the shrine and reaches the temporary shrine (御仮屋, Okariya), where attendants keep an all-night vigil. On the second day, the main procession (本祇園, Hon Gion) parades through local neighborhoods and Koduru Shopping Street (along [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 16|Prefectural Route 16]]), accompanied by children's mikoshi and festival floats.
Until [[2003]], the festival was held annually on [[July 20]] and [[July 21]], regardless of the weekday. Since [[2004]], it has been held on the Saturday and Sunday of the three-day weekend that includes [[Marine Day]] (Umi no Hi).
On the festival days, a section of Koduru Shopping Street is closed to traffic, creating a pedestrian zone from 6:00 PM to 8:00 PM on the first day and from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the second day<ref>PR poster of the festival.</ref>.
== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** ** 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** ** 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) ** 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** ** **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." ** **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. ** **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.

{{Event information | Name = Koduru Gion Festival | English name = Koduru Gion Festival | image = File:Kodurusyoutengai.jpg | image size = 280px | image caption = Procession of portable shrines and floats through Koduru Shopping Street | Type = Gion Festival (Sannō Festival) | Common name = Koduru Festival | Official name = | Former name = | Date = Saturday and Sunday of the three-day holiday weekend in July | First held = 1894 (oldest record found in Suwa Shrine documents) | Last held = | Time = | Venue = Koduru Shopping Street and surrounding area, Ibaraki Town, Higashiibaraki District, Ibaraki Prefecture | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Sponsor = | Support = | Partner = | Producer = | Number of exhibitors = | Attendance = | Venue access name = | Nearest station = [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] | Bus = From Mito Station, take the bus to "Koduru" stop. | Parking = Available | Website = | Notes = Until 2003, the festival was held annually on July 20–21. }}
The '''Koduru Gion Festival''' (小鶴祇園祭) is an annual [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]] held every July in the Koduru area of [[Ibaraki Town]], [[Higashiibaraki District]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]].
== Overview == The festival is a ritual of [[Yasaka Shrine]], which is located within the precincts of [[Suwa Shrine (Koduru)]]. It is said to have begun during the [[Meiji period]] when an [[epidemic]] spread through the area<ref>Suwa Shrine’s records show that the festival was held in [[1894]] (Meiji 27).</ref>. To pray for health and safety, a Yasaka Shrine was built within Suwa Shrine’s grounds, and the festival was initiated<ref>[http://www.nukaga-fukushiro.jp/report/098-2012/06-7-2/ Report by Fukushiro Nukaga, 2012/07/15]</ref>.
Although it declined once during the [[Taisho period]], young volunteers revived it, and since the [[1980s]], it has gained popularity once again<ref>“Ibaraki Town History - Geography Volume.”</ref>.
On the first day, the evening procession (宵祇園, Yoi Gion) begins when the portable shrine (mikoshi) leaves the shrine and reaches the temporary shrine (御仮屋, Okariya), where attendants keep an all-night vigil. On the second day, the main procession (本祇園, Hon Gion) parades through local neighborhoods and Koduru Shopping Street (along [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 16|Prefectural Route 16]]), accompanied by children's mikoshi and festival floats.
Until [[2003]], the festival was held annually on [[July 20]] and [[July 21]], regardless of the weekday. Since [[2004]], it has been held on the Saturday and Sunday of the three-day weekend that includes [[Marine Day]] (Umi no Hi).
On the festival days, a section of Koduru Shopping Street is closed to traffic, creating a pedestrian zone from 6:00 PM to 8:00 PM on the first day and from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the second day<ref>PR poster of the festival.</ref>.
== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** ** 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** ** 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) ** 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** ** **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." ** **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. ** **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

[[Category:Festivals in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Gion Festivals]]
}}

* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** ** **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." ** **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. ** **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

revision.text:
{{Event information | Name = Koduru Gion Festival | English name = Koduru Gion Festival | image = File:Kodurusyoutengai.jpg | image size = 280px | image caption = Procession of portable shrines and floats through Koduru Shopping Street | Type = Gion Festival (Sannō Festival) | Common name = Koduru Festival | Official name = | Former name = | Date = Saturday and Sunday of the three-day holiday weekend in July | First held = 1894 (oldest record found in Suwa Shrine documents) | Last held = | Time = | Venue = Koduru Shopping Street and surrounding area, Ibaraki Town, Higashiibaraki District, Ibaraki Prefecture | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Sponsor = | Support = | Partner = | Producer = | Number of exhibitors = | Attendance = | Venue access name = | Nearest station = [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] | Bus = From Mito Station, take the bus to "Koduru" stop. | Parking = Available | Website = | Notes = Until 2003, the festival was held annually on July 20–21. }}
The '''Koduru Gion Festival''' (小鶴祇園祭) is an annual [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]] held every July in the Koduru area of [[Ibaraki Town]], [[Higashiibaraki District]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]].
== Overview == The festival is a ritual of [[Yasaka Shrine]], which is located within the precincts of [[Suwa Shrine (Koduru)]]. It is said to have begun during the [[Meiji period]] when an [[epidemic]] spread through the area<ref>Suwa Shrine’s records show that the festival was held in [[1894]] (Meiji 27).</ref>. To pray for health and safety, a Yasaka Shrine was built within Suwa Shrine’s grounds, and the festival was initiated<ref>[http://www.nukaga-fukushiro.jp/report/098-2012/06-7-2/ Report by Fukushiro Nukaga, 2012/07/15]</ref>.
Although it declined once during the [[Taisho period]], young volunteers revived it, and since the [[1980s]], it has gained popularity once again<ref>“Ibaraki Town History - Geography Volume.”</ref>.
On the first day, the evening procession (宵祇園, Yoi Gion) begins when the portable shrine (mikoshi) leaves the shrine and reaches the temporary shrine (御仮屋, Okariya), where attendants keep an all-night vigil. On the second day, the main procession (本祇園, Hon Gion) parades through local neighborhoods and Koduru Shopping Street (along [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 16|Prefectural Route 16]]), accompanied by children's mikoshi and festival floats.
Until [[2003]], the festival was held annually on [[July 20]] and [[July 21]], regardless of the weekday. Since [[2004]], it has been held on the Saturday and Sunday of the three-day weekend that includes [[Marine Day]] (Umi no Hi).
On the festival days, a section of Koduru Shopping Street is closed to traffic, creating a pedestrian zone from 6:00 PM to 8:00 PM on the first day and from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the second day<ref>PR poster of the festival.</ref>.
== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** ** 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** ** 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) ** 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** ** **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." ** **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. ** **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

The '''Koduru Gion Festival''' (小鶴祇園祭) is an annual [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]] held every July in the Koduru area of [[Ibaraki Town]], [[Higashiibaraki District]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]].
== Overview == The festival is a ritual of [[Yasaka Shrine]], which is located within the precincts of [[Suwa Shrine (Koduru)]]. It is said to have begun during the [[Meiji period]] when an [[epidemic]] spread through the area<ref>Suwa Shrine’s records show that the festival was held in [[:en:1894|1894]] (Meiji 27).</ref>. To pray for health and safety, a Yasaka Shrine was built within Suwa Shrine’s grounds, and the festival was initiated<ref>[http://www.nukaga-fukushiro.jp/report/098-2012/06-7-2/ Report by Fukushiro Nukaga, 2012/07/15]</ref>.
Although it declined once during the [[Taisho period]], young volunteers revived it, and since the [[:en:1980s|1980s]], it has gained popularity once again<ref>“Ibaraki Town History - Geography Volume.”</ref>.
On the first day, the evening procession (宵祇園, Yoi Gion) begins when the portable shrine (mikoshi) leaves the shrine and reaches the temporary shrine (御仮屋, Okariya), where attendants keep an all-night vigil. On the second day, the main procession (本祇園, Hon Gion) parades through local neighborhoods and Koduru Shopping Street (along [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 16|Prefectural Route 16]]), accompanied by children's mikoshi and festival floats.
Until [[2003]], the festival was held annually on [[July 20]] and [[July 21]], regardless of the weekday. Since [[2004]], it has been held on the Saturday and Sunday of the three-day weekend that includes [[Marine Day]] (Umi no Hi).
On the festival days, a section of Koduru Shopping Street is closed to traffic, creating a pedestrian zone from 6:00 PM to 8:00 PM on the first day and from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the second day<ref>PR poster of the festival.</ref>.
== Schedule == * **Day 1 (Yoi Gion - Evening Procession)** ** 18:00 – Mikoshi departs from Suwa Shrine → Procession of mikoshi and floats → 20:00 – Arrives at the temporary shrine (Okariya) * **Day 2 (Hon Gion - Main Procession)** ** 8:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats parade through local neighborhoods → 13:00 – Arrive at Okariya (rest break) ** 18:00 – Depart from Okariya → Mikoshi and floats procession → 19:30–21:30 – Stage performances → 21:45 – Return to the shrine → 22:00 – Festival ends
* The main procession passes through various Koduru neighborhoods, including Miyamoto-cho (Shimojuku), Nakajuku, Daisan, Nishimachi, Minamimachi, Daiyon, Bakke, Daimachi, and Isshin. * Until the mid-Showa period, the main procession took place at midnight, followed by a ritual purification in the [[Hinuma River]], before returning to the shrine<ref>Exhibition of a 1954 mikoshi purification ritual photo at the "Koduru Street Gallery" event in 2002.</ref>. * Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the main procession on the second day started at 1:00 PM. However, since 2023, it begins in the morning to prevent heatstroke.
== Festival Floats == [[File:KODURUBAYASHIDASHI.jpg|thumb|Koduru-bayashi Floats – The left is the 3rd-generation float completed in 2018, the right is the 2nd-generation float from 2014.]] Two festival floats are maintained by the Koduru-bayashi Preservation Society. The performance includes a mix of large taiko drums, small taiko drums, flutes, and gongs. The upper tier of the float is used for dancing, while the lower tier is for musical performances. Dances feature [[Okame (mask)|Okame]], [[Hyottoko]], and [[Fox spirit|Kitsune]] masks.
* **1981 (Showa 56)** – The first-generation float was completed and used for 32 years before retiring in **2013**. * **2014 (Heisei 26)** – The second-generation float was completed and is used for the local parade. * **2018 (Heisei 30)** – A third-generation float, based on the first, was completed and is used for the shopping street parade.
== Mikoshi (Portable Shrines) == [[File:KAKUSAIKAIMIKOSHI.jpg|thumb|Kakusai-kai Mikoshi]] The festival features one large mikoshi for adults, one mikoshi for junior high school students, and three smaller mikoshi for children, all managed by the **Kakusai-kai (鶴祭會)** festival association.
* **1982 (Showa 57)** – The Kakusai-kai mikoshi was completed.
== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** ** **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." ** **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. ** **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />

== Access == * **Location:** Koduru Shopping Street, Ibaraki Town (along Prefectural Route 16) * **Transportation:** ** **By Train/Bus**: Take the [[JR East]] [[Jōban Line]] to [[Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]], then take a [[Kantō Railway]] bus bound for "Okuno-ya" and get off at "Koduru." ** **By Car**: From [[Kita-Kantō Expressway]] **Ibaraki Town West IC**, take [[Ibaraki Prefectural Route 59|Route 59]] and Route 16 for about 10 minutes. Temporary parking is available. ** **By Air**: From [[Ibaraki Airport]], take the [[Kantō Railway]] bus to Mito Station and get off at "Koduru."
== Additional Information == * From **1996 to 2001**, the first day featured the "Koduru Yosakoi Festival." * In **2011**, due to the effects of the [[:en:2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the mikoshi and float procession was canceled, and only a ritual was held at Suwa Shrine. The stage events were rescheduled to the **New Year of 2012**. * The stage performances include an **impressionist singing show**, **bingo tournament**, and **karaoke contest**. The karaoke contest is open to anyone who registers in advance<ref>[http://ibawakublog.blog.fc2.com/blog-entry-955.html Ibaraki Town Koduru Gion Festival Event]</ref>. * From **2020 to 2022**, the mikoshi and float procession was canceled due to COVID-19. Only a small ritual was conducted at Suwa Shrine with limited attendance. * In **2023**, the festival was held in a reduced format, with only the second day’s procession<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>. * In **2024**, the festival returned to a full two-day schedule for the first time in five years<ref name="PR">PR poster</ref>.
== Related Festivals == * [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]] * [[Ibaraki Festival]] – The Koduru Gion Festival once participated in this event before its cancellation. * [[Mito Kōmon Festival]] – The Koduru Festival's mikoshi is part of this event. * [[San'ya-san Suehiro Festival]] – The Kakusai-kai lantern mikoshi is featured annually.
== References == <references />
